source_scope: Full private PDF text extracted locally

source_anchors.what_text_says: Title, source note, and table of contents establish a full book-shaped source.
The author opens with a hospital crisis and the failure of harder work as the default answer.
The first chapter presents play as an energy source and offers experiments.
The middle section treats clarity, courage, and next actions as ways to reduce emotional and practical blockers.
The later chapters treat rest, recharge, and alignment as long-term maintenance and calibration.

baseline_summary_trap: The book says productivity improves when you feel good, play more, rest more, and run experiments.
upgrade_question: When painful effort stops working, how can a reader redesign energy, clarity, friction, fear, rest, and meaning so action becomes more sustainable without turning happiness into a universal cure?

lens_candidates.text_trigger: hospital opening and failed harder-work response
lens_candidates.clarifies: why the book begins from a breakdown rather than a productivity tactic
lens_candidates.reader_gain: reader can inspect the diagnosis before applying tactics

lens_candidates.text_trigger: play, control, connection, rest, recharge, alignment
lens_candidates.clarifies: why the book groups tactics around energy and sustainability
lens_candidates.reader_gain: reader can place tactics into a system

lens_candidates.text_trigger: book title and many feel-good tactics

absorb: Diagnose the system before blaming willpower.
Treat experiments as bounded changes with observation.
downgrade: Use feel-good tactics as context-sensitive aids, not universal cures.
reject: Do not treat happiness, play, or rest as proof that responsibility disappears.

reader_facing_explanation.title: 先读正文：这本书真正反对的是“靠痛苦证明努力”
reader_facing_explanation.body: The reader-facing page frames the book as a diagnosis shift while pointing back to the full body track.

delta_eval.reader_gain: Reader can distinguish the book's energy-system argument from generic happiness advice.
